quote: All I'm doing is trying to schedule something. So if you have a quick five minutes right now, I'll just run through it super quick, let you get back to your family, and then we can set something up for next week.
stewart_read: Carter executes the time-constraint protocol correctly: he names the time ask explicitly, frames the benefit to Shirley, and anchors the outcome. This is the call's one proactive schema-aligned move and it directly earns the five minutes Shirley was about to deny. Strategic read holds — this is not luck, it's the right move executed cleanly.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Carter was just pushing back instinctively and happened to hit the right framing. Rejected — the structure of the response (time ask + benefit + outcome anchor) matches the protocol too closely to be accidental.
coaching_implication: Use this clip as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1. Carter has the time-constraint move. The coaching question is why this discipline didn't carry through to the button-up.

stewart_read: Two months in the home is the root cause of every subsequent knowledge gap — roof age unknown, utility company unknown, bill history nonexistent. Carter captures the residence length but does not register its downstream implication: this customer cannot produce a utility bill on this call, and the closer will arrive without usage data. The verify item is checked; the implication is missed.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have recognized the bill-collection problem and deliberately skipped it to keep the call short given Shirley's time pressure. Rejected — there is no evidence Carter attempted or consciously deferred bill collection; he simply never reaches it.
coaching_implication: When residence length comes back under six months, coach Carter to flag it explicitly: 'Since you're so new, the specialist will pull your usage data directly — that's actually easier for you.' This sets the closer's expectation and removes the bill-collection gap from the handoff.

quote: No. Do I know how old it is? No. But Yeah. It's not that old. It's in my package. I'd have to go and look it up.
stewart_read: Roof age is a hard-DQ trigger if over 15 years with no replacement planned (dq_rules.roof_too_old_no_replacement). Carter accepts 'I'd have to look it up' and moves on without flagging the gap or noting it for the closer. The closer cannot complete qualification without this data — and if the roof turns out to be over 15 years, the cross-sell to roofing (cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned) becomes the only revenue path.
alternative_hypothesis: Given Shirley's time pressure, Carter may have judged that pressing for roof data would cost the call. Plausible, but the correct move is a brief flag ('I'll note that for the specialist to confirm') rather than silent omission.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to close open verify items with a forward bridge rather than silent acceptance: 'No worries — I'll flag that for the specialist so they can confirm before the appointment.' This keeps the call moving and ensures the closer knows what's missing.

quote: Oh, I don't know. I and, you know, everything's so new to me. Name something. And then, is it Las Vegas Power? I don't know. I'd have to go look that one up too.
stewart_read: Shirley's 'everything's so new to me' is not just a knowledge gap — it's a compounding new-homeowner signal. She has no bill history, no utility familiarity, and no roof data. Carter resolves the utility question by looking it up himself (a reasonable workaround) but does not register that bill collection is structurally impossible on this call. The enthusiasm signal buried here: a brand-new homeowner who filled out a solar form within two months of moving in is a high-intent lead — she's thinking about solar before she's even unpacked. Carter walks past this entirely.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to name the new-homeowner signal as a positive: 'You're already thinking about this two months in — that's exactly the right time to look at it.' This builds rapport and reframes the knowledge gaps as expected rather than disqualifying. Then bridge: 'The specialist will pull your usage data directly, so you don't need to have the bill handy.'

quote: the main requirement to get solar is having a credit score above 6 50. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Two issues compound here. First, Carter states the threshold as 650, not 670 — a schema drift flagged in qualifiers.credit_qualifier.drift_observed. Second, and more consequentially, Carter delivers the qualifier as a binary gate with no alternative path framed. When Shirley says no, Carter has nothing prepared. The schema's open policy question (should setters DQ on stated credit, or let the closer's pull decide?) is directly relevant — Shirley's stated 611 may not reflect her actual bureau score, and she immediately signals cash-purchase intent as an alternative path.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter may have intended to pivot to cash options regardless of the answer. Rejected — his 'Oh, it's not?' at 02:23 is a genuine surprise response, not a setup for a pivot.
coaching_implication: Two coaching beats for the manager: (1) Correct the threshold to 670. (2) Coach Carter to frame the qualifier with an alternative path built in: 'The financing program requires a 670, but we also have purchase options — so either way we can make it work.' This prevents the DQ from stalling the call.

quote: No. I don't think so. That's why I was getting ready to just go on and buy the system because I don't charge a lot of anything. I pay for my house with cash. I pay for my new car with cash. So I don't like monthly bills.
stewart_read: Shirley's 'I don't like monthly bills' is a textbook inversion trigger per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion. The flip available here: 'That's exactly why solar makes sense — you'd be replacing a monthly bill you'll pay forever with a one-time purchase you own.' Carter instead responds to the cash-buyer signal at 02:59 with 'That's how we do. Wanna do a cash deal?' — a competent reactive pivot that acknowledges the cash path, but he never deploys the inversion on the bill-pain statement itself. The emotional leverage in 'I don't like monthly bills' goes unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Carter's cash-deal pivot at 02:59 could be read as an implicit inversion — he's validating the cash path rather than pushing financing. Plausible as a partial move, but the inversion on the bill-pain statement would have been the higher-leverage moment and it precedes the cash pivot by 35 seconds.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the inversion framework from coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion. The suggested rephrase intent (not verbatim): acknowledge the bill aversion, then flip it — 'That's actually the best reason to look at solar: you'd be replacing a bill you pay every month forever with something you own outright.' This is the emotional anchor the closer needs set before the appointment.

quote: I have the capital. I have a black card.
stewart_read: Shirley is volunteering her financial capacity unprompted — black card, cash home purchase, cash car purchase. This is a high-value cash-buyer qualifier that the closer needs explicitly. Carter's 'That's how we do. Wanna do a cash deal?' at 02:59 acknowledges the cash path but does not capture or confirm the qualifier in a way that survives the handoff. Nothing in the button-up or any closing statement flags this for the closer.
coaching_implication: Coach Carter to verbally confirm and anchor cash-buyer signals before the button-up: 'So you'd be looking at a purchase option — I'll make sure the specialist comes prepared with that.' This both confirms the path and sets the closer's agenda. The handoff brief must carry this explicitly.

quote: Yeah. We'll send someone by to to give you the cost. Would Monday morning or evening work better for you?
stewart_read: Carter books the appointment but executes none of the five required button-up elements: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no computer/no-driving ask, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise. The booking is real but structurally bare. Additionally, 'send someone by' implies an in-person visit — if this is a phone appointment, the closer and Shirley may have mismatched expectations on format.
alternative_hypothesis: Given Shirley's stated time pressure, Carter may have judged that a full button-up would cost the booking. Plausible — but the 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/no-driving ask are 15-second additions that materially affect show rate. The time-pressure argument doesn't justify skipping them entirely.
coaching_implication: Run the button-up checklist with Carter in the 1-on-1. The five elements are not optional even under time pressure — coach him to compress them, not skip them. Flag the 'send someone by' language specifically: if this is a phone appointment, the closer needs to correct that expectation at the top of the call.
